content: Yeah there is no way to backtest options in TZ. My recommendation is to backtest with stock. author: Jdub
content: Yes I talked about this a few times. You want to focus on the setups itself and not the higher timeframe thesis. The higher timeframe thesis is something that you can add since its more discretionary and not mechinal. Something like that you cannot backtest. As for the entry models same thing there is three different entry models you can enter in off of and you can backtest each.

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
Hi <@1306252400935436364> ,

To add to what <@801169196015943731> mentioned, in summary, your backtesting should focus solely on the entry criteria without any added context other than price action. The goal is to make backtesting as mechanical as possible to ensure consistency. This means focusing specifically on how price reacts to predetermined levels, such as the break and retest zones, without considering higher timeframe theses or discretionary elements.

By isolating the entry mechanics during backtesting, you’ll be able to gather clean, unbiased data to understand the reliability of the setup itself. Once you have that foundation, you can gradually layer in confluences like higher timeframe alignment for your live trading, which introduces a more discretionary element.

Approaching backtesting in this structured way will give you a clear picture of how your setups perform and help you build confidence in your execution. 

Let us know if you have more questions—happy to help!

author: Luke
content:
With backtesting I would personally backtest the tickers that you like. If starting I'd try ones that aren't a ton of money like AAPL or AMZN. Depending on the certain setups you know, I'd backtest them one at a time. So for one backtest session I would for example backtest NVDA over the past month using strictly PDH/PDL retest, and log your results with that. Then compare it to other setups on that ticker like the 5min opening range break and retest.

Backtesting is to build confidence, and see what's been working lately. It gives you more conviction to "pull the trigger" when looking to enter a trade. Another thing is I would post your trades in https://discord.com/channels/1218766394997346395/1219021113686888478 so that you can get imput on your trades that didn't work out, so that you can be sure not to make that mistake again.

The biggest thing that has personally worked for me is asking other traders for insights on my trades, whether they're winners or losers, and writing down on paper the things that I've been doing wrong. Eventually over time you will notice that you are repeatedly making that same mistake, and that can help you really to focus on things you are doing wrong. Hope that helps

author: Aleksandra Seliverstova
content:
Hi Kam, what would be your advice on "intuitively bad setups" during backtesting? 

For example, if I feel like the ticker is choppy today, I get one loss in a long position during the first 30 min after market open, then it reverses and shows an entry into a short position. But I personally wouldn't take it based on my experience with this kind of choppy days. 

Do I have to still take it during backtesting, because this "feeling" of mine is barely repeatable?
And what do I do if most of the losses in backtesting come from this kind of setups that somehow look bad but I don't always understand why.

author: Kam
content:
Great question, Aleksandra. Here's how I approach this in the framework I'm building for my own system:

When you get a gut feel that something is off—like a choppy morning or a textbook setup that just feels wrong—that intuition might be valid. But during backtesting, I treat it as a diagnostic opportunity, not a reason to skip the trade. So yes, if it meets your setup rules, you should still take the trade.

That said, it depends a bit on your experience level.
If you're newer and just trying to learn the setups, then treat the backtest mechanically—take every valid trade to build your reps.

But if you're at the stage where you're noticing “off” conditions, you may be testing your discretionary judgment, not just the setup. In that case, try quantifying your intuition. You could try the following:

- Keep session notes
- Add a tag in your spreadsheet like “Intuition Flagged”

Over time, you'll build a dataset of “visually valid, but intuitively off” trades. That'll help you see whether your gut feel is part of a repeatable edge—or just noise.

If these end up being most of your losers, that’s your cue to refine the system. Look for disqualifiers or context filters that might show up repeatedly in those losing trades. For example:

- Was it within the first 15–30 minutes of a choppy open?
- Was HTF structure unclear or consolidating?
- Was there a liquidity grab  beforehand?

Hope this helps—this kind of reflection is where edge really starts to form!

author: Aleksandra Seliverstova
content:
Thanks a lot Kam! That's very thorough. 

Yeah that's kinda what I'm doing, trying to notice common traits in these "off setups" and create hypothesis based on them for further backtesting. Like, not taking setups under HoD / above LoD which filters most of the chop in range also killing some good setups, but overall more efficient.

It's harder with setups where I don't see anything objectively different. For example, if the structure is very stiff, still keeping it bullish or bearish, but the PA looks unnatural, popping and dropping very hard. It's like, I think to myself, that's not what I usually see when I get a good setup, but this is sooo subjective and also hard to repeat in real time trading.

But yeah, I got what you mean, thanks a lot!

author: Kam
content: all good! you're asking the exact right questions and thinking the right way imo. keep working at it, eventually you'll be able to turn that 'subjectivity' into repeatable rules or go / no-go signals. just takes time and reps (that's what i keep telling myself too) 😁

author: Kam
content: btw what you just described around structure could be gold for your backtests. create a tag like "structure intact, PA off" or something to quantify and track!

author: wakefieldcapital
content:
Few comments on this one. Great that you're backtesting, it is really a powerful tool to learn more about price action. Looking at your entry model, 1 month ago, I'd do the same thing. But now, I have learnt the process of reading price action and the market reactiveness . 

In this specific NVDA trade, rather than blindly applying the 5min ORH retest strategy, you could also add another confluence to it - order block. 
The body of the 6:34 red candle would be the order block (highest down close candle) and the SL would be at bottom of the body. Also, keep in mind that a trade looses if and only if the body of a candle closes beneath the SL. 
Entry would be during the formation of the 6:45 candle aiming for a 2R trade. 

However, if you're risk appetite is low, I'd not take this trade as the retest of the 5min ORH wasn't the best since both the retest and the confirmation candle have closed below the 5min ORH.

Hi this was a good trade with a well managed risk 🙂👍 Technically, you were never invalidated, but I can totally see why you closed this one. author: Luke
content: When you start backtesting as a beginner to the system,  the first thing you want to do is backtest the specific setups to build confidence in the specific ones (Previous Day High, Premarket High, 5min high, 1 Candle Rule etc..); you probably have heard that there is no "best setup" in terms of the level, but instead it's all based on context and HTFs as well. But once you backtest the specific setups just to build confidence in them and to keep it simple, what I would do is start marking out levels like you would for one ticker and backtest that one for the day with any setup that sets-up. Then, this builds confidence in your edge... and helps you to know that your system actually wins, but also loses under certain conditions. This helps with getting to know personalities of tickers as well. What I currently do is go to random days, and mark out basic levels on all of the tickers on my watchlist, and play what looks best on the HTFs; I'll draw arrows like my Premarket Gameplan and treat it like real-time... if you use the 1 second interval on 1 second candles, it makes it very realistic to taking the trade in real time (of course emotions aren't there). One thing I highly recommend you do is go to the live trading on a day that you backtested and took a hypothetical trade, and see if Tony and Jack were looking at it, or even took it (then if they did you can go watch the trade review as well). Overall, these are super solid ways to boost your confidence, and help you to know especially on losing streaks that your edge works over a long period of time. Hope this all makes sense... let me know if you have any questions!

author: Sean
content:
Final results of this session. I didn't get all the way to December. I guess my paramotors weren’t set up properly. But, overall, I am pleased with my results.  Profit factor could use some improvement. This could be due to me cutting my winners short and/or selling 50% off at 1:1R. My main take away from all this is that PDH/L levels are great retest levels and should be (for me) utilized every time my alerts get triggered on the indices. 

Another thing I could improve would be where my stop placement is going to be. I continue to shift back and forth between the retest candle and the break of structure level. This is because sometimes I believe the structure level is more important than the retest candle. If I do decide to put my stop there, I should adjust my position size to a smaller amount because sometimes it’s a lot larger risk. Another take away is sticking to this only, yeilds less trades. I'm thinking, you don't really need to take that many trades every month. If I took the same trades, but with larger position size I may have yielded more profits, but the trade off would have been a larger risk my nervous system would have to get used to.. 


Next up! 5min high / low BnR.

I built a TradingView indicator called Relative Strength Scanner and would love some feedback from the community.

The idea is simple: add 10–15 tickers you’re monitoring, and the indicator automatically sorts them by their relative strength since the market open. It helps quickly identify the strongest and weakest names in your watchlist without constantly flipping through charts or setting multiple alerts.

You can check it out here:
https://www.tradingview.com/script/bbdkueTo-Relative-Strength-Scanner/

I’d love to hear your thoughts and any suggestions for improvement.
Thanks!

author: Metamorphosis
content:
<@613464484350132250> ,I’ve had a look, and the concept looks really promising.

One feature that would make it even more powerful is the ability to include multiple indexes in the table. Traders like myself often use both SPY and QQQ correlation to gauge the strength and direction of the market.

It would also be great to have the ability to filter stocks based on whether they're above or below key levels such as PDH/PDL, PMH/PML, etc., with the option to apply these filters simultaneously to narrow down only the stocks that meet all the selected criteria.

Another useful addition would be the ability to customise the ORB timeframe to whatever the user prefers. It would also be helpful to filter stocks based on whether price is above or below the ORB range, with a simple visual indicator in the table, similar to the PDH/PDL symbols.

Finally, it would be great to include VWAP and EMA/SMA in the table, again using simple symbols to show whether price is above or below each level, along with the ability to apply those filters simultaneously.

Overall, I think the concept is excellent. These additions would make it an incredibly powerful tool for traders who use index correlation, key price levels, ORB, VWAP, and moving averages to identify high-probability setups.

Thanks for your effort.

author: kalerv
content:
Hey, thanks for the detailed feedback — genuinely useful to hear how other traders are thinking about this.

Before jumping into building, I want to make sure anything added actually improves decisions rather than just adding more to look at. A few honest questions:

On multiple benchmarks: When SPY and QQQ are pointing in different directions on a given day, what do you actually do differently? Do you trade both signals, pick the stronger one, or sit out entirely? Asking because two RS columns that sometimes disagree might create more confusion than clarity — want to understand your actual process before wiring it in.

On ORB timeframe: What timeframe do you use and why that one specifically? And when price is sitting between the ORB high and low, do you pass on the trade or just note it as context? If it’s a hard filter for you, that’s worth building. If it’s just nice to see, it probably doesn’t need its own column.

On VWAP/EMA: Can you walk me through a recent trade where VWAP or an EMA specifically changed your decision — not just confirmed it, but actually changed it? The concern here is that these are session-anchor or HTF references, and this tool is designed to find intraday relative strength right now. Adding HTF filters risks answering a different question than the one the tool is built for.

On filtering: Agreed it sounds useful, but a stock that’s “Inside” at 9:40 and filtered out might be your best trade at 10:15. The color coding already lets you scan for what meets criteria without hiding names you might need later.

The core goal of this tool is one thing: at any point during the session, which names are genuinely leading or lagging the market right now — not just historically, not just structurally, but in the tape. Everything in scope needs to serve that question directly.

What does your actual pre-trade checklist look like step by step? That would help figure out where this fits and what, if anything, is genuinely missing.
